cluster.title: Unit Conversion Tools — Kefiw
cluster.subhead: Length, weight, temperature, volume, area, speed, time.
cluster.intro: The standard conversion toolbox with a clean mobile layout. Each tool below supports both directions and a full set of common units.
cluster.description: Unit converters for length, weight, temperature, volume, area, speed, and time — plus quick-reference guides for the most common conversions.

faq.a: Yes. Enter decimals or fractions (as decimals) and convert freely.
Results are displayed to 6 significant figures by default — enough precision for everyday use without noise.

articles.title: Common Length Conversions — Inches, Feet, Metres, and Miles
articles.subhead: The conversion factors worth memorising, and mental-math shortcuts for the rest.

current.description: The most common length conversions — inches, feet, yards, miles, metres, kilometres — with factors and mental-math tricks.
current.outcomeLine: 1 inch = 2.54 cm. 1 foot = 30.48 cm. 1 mile ≈ 1.61 km. Almost everything else derives from these.
current.intro: Length conversions come up constantly — recipes, furniture, travel, fitness. Four or five factors cover almost every real case.
keyPoints: 1 inch = 2.54 cm (exactly, by international agreement in 1959).
1 foot = 12 inches = 30.48 cm ≈ 0.3 metres.
1 yard = 3 feet = 0.9144 metres.
1 mile = 1.609 km. Mental math: miles × 1.6, or miles + (miles × 0.6).
1 metre ≈ 3.28 feet ≈ 39 inches. 1 km ≈ 0.62 miles.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — any length unit to any other, with precision controls.
For room sizes, plots of land — once length matters in two dimensions.

faq.q: Is inch exactly 2.54 cm or is it approximate?
faq.a: Exactly. Since 1959, the international inch is defined as exactly 2.54 cm. Before that it was a fraction off, which is why old conversions sometimes disagree at the fifth decimal.

faq.q: Why do UK miles and US miles equal?
faq.a: Both use the international mile (1609.344 m exactly). The UK also uses the statute mile for some surveying — same value. US survey miles are slightly different but rarely encountered.

notes_for_writer: Keep the existing structured fields but tighten/expand as needed.

articles.title: Fahrenheit vs. Celsius — The Formula and Mental Shortcuts
articles.subhead: The exact formula, and the quick approximation that gets you within 2°.

current.description: Fahrenheit to Celsius and back — exact formula, mental-math approximation, and reference temperatures.
current.outcomeLine: C = (F − 32) × 5/9. Quick estimate: subtract 30 and halve, or double and add 30.
current.intro: The temperature conversion formula is simple but awkward to do in your head. A close approximation lets you convert weather or body temperatures to within a degree or two without a calculator.
keyPoints: Quick F → C: subtract 30, halve. (68 − 30) ÷ 2 = 19 (actual: 20).
Quick C → F: double, add 30. 20 × 2 + 30 = 70 (actual: 68).
Approximation error: usually 1–2°. Fine for weather, not for cooking or medical.
Reference points worth memorising: 0°C = 32°F (freezing), 100°C = 212°F (boiling), 37°C = 98.6°F (body).

examples.title: Weather: 25°C
examples.body: Exact: 25 × 9/5 + 32 = 77°F. Quick: 25 × 2 + 30 = 80°F. 3° off — fine for "is it warm".

examples.title: Oven: 350°F
examples.body: Exact: (350 − 32) × 5/9 = 177°C. Most UK ovens have a 180°C setting — close enough.

examples.title: Fever: 101°F
examples.body: Exact: (101 − 32) × 5/9 = 38.3°C. Quick: (101 − 30) ÷ 2 = 35.5°C — big error at high F. Use exact for medical.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — exact conversion with decimal precision.
Often paired when converting recipe units (oven temp + dish size).

faq.q: Why is the quick approximation inaccurate at high temperatures?
faq.a: The formula C = (F − 32) × 5/9 uses 5/9 ≈ 0.556, but the quick version uses 0.5. The error scales with the temperature difference from 32°F.

faq.q: Is there a cross-over point where F = C?
faq.a: Yes: −40°. Minus 40 Fahrenheit equals minus 40 Celsius exactly. Useful trivia, not much else.

articles.title: Litres vs. Gallons — And the US/UK Gallon Trap
articles.subhead: One litre, two different gallons — know which one you are dealing with.

current.description: Volume conversions between litres and gallons, with the critical US vs. UK gallon difference.
current.outcomeLine: 1 US gallon = 3.785 L. 1 UK gallon = 4.546 L. They are NOT the same size.
current.intro: The gallon is two different sizes depending on where you are. A US gallon is about 20% smaller than a UK gallon — so "fuel economy in mpg" or "tank capacity in gallons" mean different things in the two countries.
keyPoints: 1 US gallon = 3.785 litres (128 US fluid ounces).
1 UK (imperial) gallon = 4.546 litres (160 UK fluid ounces).
1 UK gallon ≈ 1.2 US gallons. That is a ~20% difference.
A UK fluid ounce and a US fluid ounce are also different — UK fl oz is slightly smaller.
Fuel economy: 30 mpg (UK) ≈ 25 mpg (US). Always check which gallon.

examples.title: Recipe: "one gallon"
examples.body: American recipe = 3.785 L. British recipe = 4.546 L. Assume US unless stated otherwise in modern cookbooks.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — supports US gallons, UK gallons, litres, millilitres, and more.

faq.q: Why are US and UK gallons different?
faq.a: Historical accident. The UK imperial gallon was defined in 1824 based on 10 lb of water. The US kept the older English wine gallon (231 cubic inches). Neither country has changed since.

faq.q: Which gallon do Canada and Australia use?
faq.a: Both use metric officially. Canada historically used UK gallons; Australia too. But for fuel, anything pre-metric in those countries is ambiguous — check.

articles.title: MPH vs. KM/H — The Quick Mental Conversion
articles.subhead: Multiply by 1.6 or divide by 1.6 — and two shortcuts that are close enough.

current.description: Converting miles per hour to kilometres per hour and back — exact factor and mental-math shortcuts for driving.
current.outcomeLine: 1 mph = 1.609 km/h. Quick: multiply by 1.6, or add 60% of the number.
current.intro: Speed conversion comes up whenever you cross a border between mph-countries (US, UK) and km/h-countries (most of the rest of the world). One factor and a mental-math trick cover it.
keyPoints: 1 mph = 1.609 km/h. 1 km/h = 0.621 mph.
Quick mph → km/h: mph × 1.6, or mph + 60% of mph.
Quick km/h → mph: km/h × 0.6, or km/h − 40%.
Useful reference points: 100 km/h ≈ 62 mph. 60 mph ≈ 96 km/h.
Speed limits you will see: UK motorway 70 mph = 113 km/h. German autobahn advisory 130 km/h ≈ 81 mph.

examples.title: Marathon pace: 10 km/h
examples.body: 10 × 0.6 = 6 mph. A 10-km/h pace is about a 10-minute mile.

whenToUse.note: Main tool — supports mph, km/h, m/s, knots.
When converting distance alongside speed (trip planning).

faq.q: Why do US and UK both use mph?
faq.a: Historical inertia. Both countries went partially metric but kept miles for road signs. The UK uses km for most other measurements; the US mostly does not.

faq.q: Is the conversion for speed limits exact?
faq.a: Close but not exact — 70 mph = 112.65 km/h, but signs round to 110 or 112. For legal enforcement, the local unit is authoritative.

articles.title: Metric vs. Imperial — The Short Survival Guide
articles.subhead: Which countries use which, and the minimum set of conversions for travellers and readers.

current.description: A practical guide to metric and imperial systems — which countries use what, the key conversion factors, and when each one is easier to work with.
current.outcomeLine: Almost every country uses metric officially. Three holdouts — US, Liberia, Myanmar — still use imperial as primary.
current.intro: Almost the entire world uses metric. Three countries — the US, Liberia, and Myanmar — still use imperial officially, and the UK uses a mixture (metric for weight and length in most contexts, imperial for roads and beer).
keyPoints: Length: 1 inch = 2.54 cm. 1 ft ≈ 0.3 m. 1 mile ≈ 1.6 km.
Weight: 1 lb ≈ 0.454 kg. 1 kg ≈ 2.2 lb. 1 stone = 14 lb = 6.35 kg.
Volume: 1 US gal = 3.785 L. 1 UK gal = 4.546 L. 1 L ≈ 33.8 US fl oz.
Temperature: °C = (°F − 32) × 5/9. Reference: 0°C = 32°F, 37°C = 98.6°F (body).
UK quirk: weight in stone-and-pounds for people, kg for food. Height in feet-and-inches or cm.

examples.title: Recipe: 1 cup flour
examples.body: US cup = 240 ml ≈ 125 g flour. UK recipes use grams directly — cups are an American convention.

whenToUse.note: Length across systems — feet, inches, metres, miles, kilometres.
Weight across pounds, kilograms, stones, ounces.
Volume including the US vs. UK gallon distinction.
Fahrenheit ↔ Celsius, with decimal precision for medical or scientific use.

faq.q: Why does the UK use a mix?
faq.a: The UK officially went metric in the 1960s–70s but never fully switched consumer habits. Road signs, beer, and body weight stayed imperial; food, weather, and science went metric.
For arithmetic yes — everything scales by 10. But imperial units (foot, inch, pound) are often more human-sized for everyday measurements, which is part of why they persist.

enhancement_brief.goal: Lift each article from short-form summary to authority-grade long-form. Target 1,200+ words per article while keeping the structured fields crisp.
per_article_outputs: 1. Fill `enhanced` with any structured-field overrides (usually: tighter intro, expanded keyPoints, more examples, richer FAQ).
2. Fill `longform_markdown` with 800-1200 word prose body (markdown, H2 sections only — no H1).
3. Do not modify `current` — that is the baseline snapshot for diffing.

rules: Each question must read like a real search query — not marketing-speak.
First sentence of answer is a complete standalone 15-25 word definition/answer (Google quotes it directly).
Full answer 40-60 words. Tight. Front-load the answer.
Cover distinct intents: definition, comparison, edge-case, how-to, troubleshooting. Not 3 rewordings of the same angle.
Include one target keyword in at least half the questions.
Add a faq_intent tag per entry: "definition" | "comparison" | "edge-case" | "how-to" | "troubleshooting" | "cost" | "trust"
faq_rules.target_count: 5-7 entries per article, 4-6 per tool page

structure: ## H2 section 1 — deeper "what is it" / definition with context
## H2 section 2 — the mechanic / how it works (with one small example)
## H2 section 3 — when to use vs when to avoid
## H2 section 4 — worked example or walkthrough
## H2 section 6 — tool-specific tips (link out to the tool)
longform_rules.tone: Concrete, useful, first-person-plural light. No filler. No "in conclusion". Short paragraphs.
links: Every time you mention a sibling tool in this cluster, link it with the exact URL from this brief (tools[].url).
Every time you mention a sibling article, link it with the exact URL from this brief (articles[].url).
Do not invent URLs or link to external competitors.
forbidden: Medical/legal/financial advice language ("you should", "we recommend") — use neutral framing.
Generic SEO boilerplate ("in today's fast-paced world").

internal_linking.rule: Use only URLs that appear in this JSON. Prefer linking to sibling tools/articles in THIS cluster over outbound links.

quality_gates: No fabricated facts — if uncertain, phrase as "in practice" or remove.
Every H2 section earns its space — cut if it restates the intro.
Zero AI-tells: "it is important to note", "in conclusion", "let us delve", "furthermore".
